content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12738305/", "title": "Intermittent fasting improves metabolic outcomes in adults with metabolic syndrome: a systematic review and meta-analysis", "summary": "This 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the effects of intermittent fasting (IF) on glycemic control, lipid profile, and inflammatory markers in adults with metabolic syndrome. The authors searched PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, Scopus, and Web of Science through September 2025, registered the protocol with PROSPERO (CRD420251142741), and used Cochrane RoB2 and GRADE to assess evidence quality. Ten randomized controlled trials encompassing 701 participants met inclusion criteria (adults >18 with metabolic syndrome; all IF types except Islamic fasting; placebo/control comparator). Pooled results showed statistically significant reductions in fasting blood sugar (SMD -0.51, 95% CI -0.81 to -0.20, p=0.001), insulin (SMD -0.27, p=0.027), HOMA-IR (SMD -0.39, p=0.004), HbA1c (SMD -0.25, p=0.034), LDL-C (SMD -0.34, p=0.001), and IL-6 (SMD -0.30, p=0.029). GRADE rated evidence HIGH quality for FBS, insulin, HOMA-IR, HbA1c, total cholesterol, LDL-C, and IL-6. Sensitivity analyses confirmed robustness and Egger's/Begg's tests detected no publication bias. The authors conclude IF favorably impacts the metabolic panel in adults with metabolic syndrome.", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Methodological", "Public_Health"], "key_facts": ["10 RCTs with 701 adults with metabolic syndrome included; searches through September 2025", "Fasting blood sugar reduction: SMD -0.51 (95% CI -0.81 to -0.20, p=0.001)", "LDL-C reduction: SMD -0.34 (95% CI -0.53 to -0.14, p=0.001); IL-6 reduction: SMD -0.30 (p=0.029)", "HbA1c reduction: SMD -0.25 (95% CI -0.49 to -0.02, p=0.034); HOMA-IR: SMD -0.39 (p=0.004)", "GRADE quality rated HIGH for FBS, insulin, HOMA-IR, HbA1c, TC, LDL-C, IL-6; PROSPERO CRD420251142741"], "knowledge_contribution": "Provides recent (2025), GRADE-rated high-quality meta-analytic evidence that IF produces clinically relevant, statistically significant improvements in glycemic, lipid, and inflammatory markers for adults with metabolic syndrome, strengthening the benefit side of the benefit-risk ledger with quantified effect sizes and confidence intervals from RCTs only."}, {"url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10253889/", "title": "Effects of Intermittent Fasting on Regulation of Metabolic Homeostasis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis", "summary": "This systematic review and meta-analysis compared Alternate-Day Fasting (ADF), Time-Restricted Fasting (TRF), and Religious Fasting (RF) across obesity, type 2 diabetes (T2D), and metabolic syndrome (MetS) populations. Searches covered PubMed, Scopus, Trip Database, Web of Knowledge, and Embase through June 2022, yielding 64 qualifying reports for qualitative analysis and 47 for quantitative synthesis (n=4,052 participants; 2,007 men, 1,977 women, 68 undisclosed). The review stratifies four IF sub-protocols: ADF, modified ADF, TRF, and RF, differentiated by fasting window length (12-20 hours daily) or whole-day fasts (1-4 days/week with 60-100% energy restriction, 300-600 kcal). The principal finding is that ADF protocols produced the greatest improvements in dysregulated metabolic conditions relative to TRF and RF. Obese and MetS individuals derived the largest benefits via improved adiposity, lipid homeostasis, and blood pressure. For T2D, IF effects were more limited but concentrated on insulin homeostasis, consistent with the primary metabolic dysfunction in that population. The review emphasizes that IF responses depend heavily on baseline health status and disease type, cautioning against one-size-fits-all recommendations.", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Methodological", "Public_Health", "Emerging_Trends"], "key_facts": ["64 reports qualified for qualitative analysis; 47 entered quantitative meta-analysis; search cutoff June 2022", "4,052 total participants (2,007 men, 1,977 women, 68 undisclosed) across studies", "19 studies targeted T2D, 35 targeted obese/overweight, 7 MetS, 6 healthy controls", "Four IF sub-protocols defined: ADF, modified ADF (mADF), TRF (12-20h eating windows), Religious Fasting", "ADF delivered the largest benefits for adiposity, lipid homeostasis, and blood pressure in obese/MetS cohorts"], "knowledge_contribution": "Adds a comparative dimension by showing benefits of IF are not uniform across protocols or populations: ADF outperforms TRF and RF, obese/MetS individuals benefit most, and T2D patients see narrower but targeted insulin-related improvements. This nuances the generic 'IF is beneficial' narrative and highlights baseline health status as a key moderator for personalizing fasting interventions."}]}
